summary: AI labs will acquire small-cap software companies as cheap distribution channels, gutting headcount
summary_extended: Baker argues that AI labs will pursue an acquisition strategy targeting small-cap software companies trading at 3-4x sales, using them as cheap distribution channels into enterprise customers while replacing the majority of human employees with AI. He draws an explicit historical parallel to Larry Ellison's post-dot-com consolidation playbook, where Oracle acquired PeopleSoft and Siebel to gain customer bases rather than technology, then rationalized costs aggressively. Baker's thesis implies that small-cap SaaS companies are structurally undervalued as acquisition targets because their customer relationships and distribution networks are worth more to an AI lab than their standalone earnings power. The investment implication is that small-cap software names at 3-4x revenue multiples represent potential M&A targets, though Baker notes the absence of such deals to date as evidence the opportunity has not yet been acted upon. No specific acquirers or targets are named, making this a thematic/structural call rather than a stock-specific thesis.
detail: Baker draws an explicit parallel to Larry Ellison's post-dot-com acquisition strategy (PeopleSoft, Siebel). He argues that with small-cap software valuations at 3-4x sales, AI labs can acquire these companies, replace most employees with AI, and use the customer base as a distribution channel. He notes traditional software firms have not yet made such purchases despite the opportunity.
confidence_evidence: Framed as a prediction/thesis rather than a current observation; Baker notes the absence of such deals as evidence the opportunity exists
quote_fragment: a lab can acquire one of these small software companies take out most of the humans
